systems.name: Chord-Scale Pairing System
systems.summary: Central system of the book. Every chord type belongs to a named group, and each group is paired with one or more scales. The major scale is the universal reference; all other scales are defined as transformations of it. A chord 'takes' a scale when that scale sounds good over it.

traversal_rules.name: Run-to-Dominant-Group Synchronization
traversal_rules.summary: Runs containing the 3rd suit Group 1 dominants; runs containing the 11th suit Group 2 dominants; both/neither work over either group.

references.quote_excerpt: if a run contains the 3rd, use it over any of the group-1 dominants

traversal_rules.name: Root-Based Scale Internalization
traversal_rules.summary: Dominant 7th and Minor 7th scales must be internalized from their own root; the relative-major transposition shortcut is prohibited.

references.quote_excerpt: Don't fall for the 'oh, dom. 7th scales, they're really just major scales up a 4th-trap'

references.topic: Warning: don't think m7 as relative major
references.quote_excerpt: don't think of the m7 scale in this way now

traversal_rules.name: Overtone Dominant Categorical Boundary
traversal_rules.summary: The Overtone Dominant scale is categorically excluded from the altered dominant family; its #11 is a natural overtone, not a chromatic alteration.

references.quote_excerpt: The Overtone Dominant scale is not considered to be an altered dominant scale

traversal_rules.name: Altered Dominant Package-Deal Positional Learning
traversal_rules.summary: All three altered dominant types are presented juxtaposed in the same fingerboard position so their color differences can be absorbed comparatively.

references.quote_excerpt: absorb them all in a kind of 'package deal', one position at a time

modification_rules.name: Scale-to-Chord Compatibility Rule
modification_rules.summary: Any chord containing some combination of (and only) the chord tones of a given scale can take that scale.

references.quote_excerpt: Any chord containing some combination of any of (but only) these chord tones, can take the Dominant 7th scale

modification_rules.name: Altered Dominant Tone Requirement
modification_rules.summary: A scale qualifies as an altered dominant only if it contains at least one of: b9, #9, b5, or #5(b13).

references.quote_excerpt: any dominant 7th scale with one or more of the following tones: b9, #9, b5 or #5
Any Dom. 7th type chord that contains any one or more of the four altered tones

modification_rules.name: Type 3 Altered Dominant Chord Substitution
modification_rules.summary: Over Type 3 altered dominant exercises, A7+ may substitute for A7#9+ and D49 may substitute for D6/9.

references.quote_excerpt: you may wish to try A7+ for A7#9+ and D49 for D6/9

modification_rules.name: #9 Substitution or Addition Rule
modification_rules.summary: The altered #9 may substitute for or be played alongside the natural 9 in altered dominant contexts.

references.quote_excerpt: INSTEAD OF OR IN ADDITION TO THE #9

modification_rules.name: Chord Form Visualization Prerequisite
modification_rules.summary: When a player cannot visualize scalar runs, return to the chord forms for that position before proceeding.

references.quote_excerpt: go back and learn the chord forms (group 4) for this position

modification_rules.name: Lydian Dominant Upper-Structure Triad (Polytonality)
modification_rules.summary: The 9, #11, and 13 of the Lydian Dominant scale form a major triad rooted a whole step above the chord root — the upper-structure triad of polytonal Lydian Dominant color.

references.quote_excerpt: the 9, #11 and 13 form a major triad whose root is a whole step above the root of the scale
This type of thinking and sound is commonly known as POLYTONALITY

systems.name: Fingerboard Position and Register System
systems.summary: Governs physical navigation. Position continuity keeps the player in a 5-6 fret area across chord changes. Fingerboard visualization is the primary internalization practice. Register is a compositional variable.

traversal_rules.name: Stay-in-Position Multi-Chord Rule
traversal_rules.summary: Know enough positions of each scale to remain within a 5-6 fret area across all chords in a progression.

references.quote_excerpt: stay in one little 5 or 6 fret area and play through the whole song

traversal_rules.name: Alternate Position Practice
traversal_rules.summary: Each set of runs should also be fingered in at least one alternate position.

references.quote_excerpt: Try fingering all these Em9 runs in the 5th position too

traversal_rules.name: Octave-Higher Same-Position Practice
traversal_rules.summary: Every pattern should be tried an octave higher within the same fingerboard position.

references.quote_excerpt: Also try this run an octave higher in the same position

traversal_rules.name: Arpeggio String-Set Flexibility
traversal_rules.summary: Arpeggio tones may be voiced on alternate strings (e.g., 5th & 4th strings).

references.quote_excerpt: These notes may be played on other strings too (5th & 4th strings)

modification_rules.name: Register Governs Extension Color
modification_rules.summary: The perceptual weight or spice of b7 and 9 is determined by register.

references.quote_excerpt: b7 and 9 really start to come alive in this 'higher' register

modification_rules.name: Fingerboard Visualization as Primary Practice
modification_rules.summary: Visualizing the notes on the fingerboard is identified as the single most important technique for internalizing scales.

references.quote_excerpt: VISUALIZE the notes on the fingerboard... THIS IS VERY IMPORTANT

modification_rules.name: Type 3 Area-4A Structural Boundary
modification_rules.summary: No Type 3 altered dominant material exists for fingerboard Area 4A; an instrument-specific constraint.

references.quote_excerpt: There won't be any Type 3 - 4A.. it's just the way the instrument works out

systems.name: Rhythmic and Articulation System
systems.summary: Governs time and texture: the 8th note is the baseline jazz unit; swing feel transforms straight 8ths into triplet subdivisions; double-timing introduces 16ths; trills decorate rather than substitute; clean playing must precede tempo increases.

traversal_rules.name: 8th Note as Primary Jazz Unit
traversal_rules.summary: The eighth note is jazz's normal rhythmic unit at medium and fast tempos.

references.quote_excerpt: the normal unit of rhythm in jazz is the 8th note

traversal_rules.name: Both-Feels Practice Rule
traversal_rules.summary: Every 8th-note run in the book must be practiced in both swing feel and straight feel.

references.quote_excerpt: all the 8th notes in this book should be played as jazz 8ths and straight 8ths

traversal_rules.name: Clean Playing Before Tempo Increase
traversal_rules.summary: Tempo may only be increased after clean execution is achieved at the current tempo.

references.quote_excerpt: making sure you are playing cleanly at any tempo before moving on to a faster one

traversal_rules.name: Trill Placement on Strong Beat
traversal_rules.summary: The trill is more commonly placed on the strong part of the beat than the weak.

references.quote_excerpt: The trill is more commonly used on the strong part of the beat than the weak one

modification_rules.name: Trill as Decoration, Not Substitution
modification_rules.summary: A trill does not replace the decorated note; the underlying note is held and enhanced.

references.quote_excerpt: A is not really being replaced, but instead, is just being decorated

modification_rules.name: Hammer-On Scale-Tone Constraint
modification_rules.summary: At this stage, hammer-on targets must be drawn from the scale currently in use.

references.quote_excerpt: This higher note, for now, will be found in the scale you are working with at that moment

modification_rules.name: Double-Timing as Texture Escalation
modification_rules.summary: Double-timing (16th notes) is an advanced texture layer above baseline 8th-note texture.

references.quote_excerpt: Players who possess great technique often love to break into 16th notes at medium tempos
